Maladaptive Strategies.value: try hard not think about the trauma
keep mind occupied all the time
I try hard to control my emotions

Threat.value: Having strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame
Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively
Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm

Memory.value: I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.
When I think about the frightening event I can sometimes smell things that I smelt when the frightening event happened.

Negative Appraisals.value: The event happened because of the way I acted
I will not be able to control my anger and will do something terrible

issues.explanation: For a terrorism-related trauma, accident discussions can be a plausible but indirect cue (e.g., hearing about crashes in a heightened threat context), so this is not clearly incoherent enough to flag.

No clear contradiction with terrorism trauma; babies/children can be emotionally salient triggers in many trauma survivors, especially if the event involved protecting others or loss in a public setting.
This is compatible with avoidance/hypervigilance and social discomfort after trauma; not internally contradictory.
Alcohol use is a common coping strategy in PTSD and can coexist with the other avoidance strategies listed.
Consistent with avoidance, shame, or hyperarousal; coherent with the rest of the profile.

issues.item: Stay Near Exit: make sure to stay close to exit
issues.explanation: This fits threat monitoring and escape-oriented avoidance, which are coherent with the threat and trigger items.

issues.item: Physical Reactivity: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
issues.explanation: Coherent with the trigger and memory items; no contradiction.

issues.item: Flashbacks: Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again
issues.explanation: Coherent with the reliving and photographic-memory items.

issues.item: Startle Response: Feeling jumpy or easily startled
issues.explanation: Fits hyperarousal and is internally consistent with the rest of the threat symptoms.

issues.item: Visual Memories: My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
issues.explanation: Compatible with vivid trauma recall; no internal conflict with the other memory items.

issues.item: Reliving Event: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
issues.explanation: Consistent with flashbacks and intrusive reliving.

issues.item: Photographic Recall: Remembering what happened during the frightening event is just like looking at photographs of it in my mind.
issues.explanation: This is essentially redundant with visual memories and reliving, but not contradictory.

issues.item: Self-Doubt: I can't trust that I will do the right thing
issues.explanation: Plausible post-trauma negative appraisal and consistent with avoidance/safety behaviors.

issues.item: Fear of Harm: You can never know who will harm you
issues.explanation: Highly coherent with terrorism trauma and hypervigilance.

issues.item: Lost Emotions: I will never be able to feel normal emotions again
issues.explanation: Compatible with chronic PTSD-related emotional numbing/detachment; not contradictory.

vignette:
She is a 66-year-old South Asian widow who presents with persistent post-traumatic distress following a terrorist incident. Her PCL-5 score of 38 suggests clinically significant symptoms that still shape much of her daily life. Since the event, she has been repeatedly unsettled by ordinary cues in the environment, especially the smell of alcohol, the burnt smell of smoke or scorched material, and the sound of cars. These reminders pull the event sharply into the foreground and are followed by intense fear, horror, anger, guilt, and shame, along with periods of irritability and angry outbursts. At times she also reports taking unnecessary risks, almost as if she is moving against her own caution.

The memory of the event remains fragmented and sensory rather than fully verbal. She says she cannot seem to put the frightening experience into words, and when memories surface she sometimes hears the sounds she heard at the time and smells the odours that were present during the attack. These intrusions keep the trauma feeling immediate and hard to file away as something past. She also describes strong negative views of herself, including that the event happened because of the way she acted and that she is a weak person. She fears that if she becomes angry she may not be able to control it and could do something terrible.

In response, she tries hard not to think about the trauma, keeps her mind occupied all the time, and makes a strenuous effort to control her emotions. These strategies appear to consume much of her energy, yet they leave her feeling more tense and less able to settle. Her strong emotions and sense of inner threat then feed back into the belief that she is weak and dangerous when upset, tightening the cycle.

She remains vulnerable to reminders in everyday life, and the meanings she gives to the trauma also make those reminders more potent. The smell of alcohol, burnt odours, and passing cars now carry a heavy emotional charge, and each encounter seems to reopen the same fear-laden memory. The overall picture is one of a woman who is trying hard to hold herself together, but whose day-to-day life is narrowed by intrusive sensory recollections, self-blame, and a constant effort to suppress distress.
